options.explanation: When the copper electrode is connected to the standard hydrogen electrode, it acts as the cathode and its standard electrode potential can be measured. E o = E o R - E o L = E o R - O = E o R Pt(s) | H 2 (g, 1 bar)| H + (aq., 1M) || Cu 2+ (aq., 1M)| Cu will measure standard electrode potential of copper electrode. To calculate the standard electrode potential of the given cell it is coupled with the standard hydrogen electrode in which pressure of hydrogen gas is one bar and the concentration of H + ion in the solution is one molar and also the concentrations of the oxidized and the reduced forms of the species in the right-hand half-cell are unity. Hydrogen gas can reduce copper under standard conditions.

question_text: While charging the lead storage battery ________.
question_html: While charging the lead storage battery ________.

options.text: PbSO 4 anode is reduced to Pb.
options.html: PbSO4 anode is reduced to Pb.
options.explanation: While charging the lead storage battery the reaction occurring on the cell is reversed and PbSO 4 on anode and cathode is converted into Pb and PbO 2 respectively. Hence, option PbSO 4 anode is reduced to Pb is the correct choice. The cell reactions when the battery is used are as follows: At cathode {tex}\mathrm{PbSO}_{4}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Pb}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex} (aq) (Reduction) At anode {tex}\mathrm{PbSO}_{4}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{PbO}_{2}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}{/tex} (Oxidation) Overal reaction {tex}2 \mathrm{PbSO}_{4}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{Pb}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{PbO}_{2}(\mathrm{s})+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\text {aq. })+2 \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}(\text { aq. }){/tex} .

options.text: PbSO 4 cathode is reduced to Pb.
options.html: PbSO4 cathode is reduced to Pb.

options.text: PbSO 4 cathode is oxidised to Pb.
options.html: PbSO4 cathode is oxidised to Pb.

options.text: PbSO 4 anode is oxidised to PbO 2 .
options.html: PbSO4 anode is oxidised to PbO2.

correct_option_text: PbSO 4 anode is reduced to Pb.

options.text: {tex}\mathrm{Na}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}(s) ; E_{\mathrm{Cell}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = -2.71 V
options.html: {tex}\mathrm{Na}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}(s) ; E_{\mathrm{Cell}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = -2.71 V

options.text: {tex}2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l) \longrightarrow \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{g})+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} ; E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = -1.23 V
options.html: {tex}2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l) \longrightarrow \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{g})+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} ; E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = -1.23 V

options.html: {tex}\mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{g}) ; E_{\mathrm{Cell}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = 0.00 V

options.html: {tex}\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{e}^{-} ; E_{\mathrm{Cell}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = 1.36V
options.explanation: During electrolysis of aqueous {tex}\mathrm{NaCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}\left(\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=-2.71 \mathrm{V}\right){/tex} {tex}\mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}\left(\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\mathrm{o}}=0.00 \mathrm{V}\right){/tex} At cathode, {tex}\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}{/tex} At the anode, two reactions are possible as following: {tex}\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+\mathrm{e}^{-} ; \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\mathrm{o}}=1.36 \mathrm{V}{/tex} ......(i) {tex}2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}_{2}+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} ; \mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{0}=1.23 \mathrm{V}{/tex} .......(ii) The reaction at the anode with a lower value of E o​​​​​ cell should be preferred, but oxidation of O 2 is a kinetically slow process and needs high voltage thus reaction (i) takes place.

correct_option_text: {tex}\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{e}^{-} ; E_{\mathrm{Cell}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = 1.36V

question_text: The positive value of the standard electrode potential of Cu 2+ /Cu indicates that ________. this redox couple is a stronger reducing agent than the H + /H 2 couple. this redox couple is a stronger oxidising agent than H + /H 2 . Cu can displace H 2 from acid. Cu cannot displace H 2 from acid.
question_html:
The positive value of the standard electrode potential of Cu2+/Cu indicates that ________.

  1. this redox couple is a stronger reducing agent than the H+ /H2 couple.
  2. this redox couple is a stronger oxidising agent than H+ /H2.
  3. Cu can displace H2 from acid.
  4. Cu cannot displace H2 from acid.
solution_text: The Lesser the E' value of redox couple, the higher the reducing power {tex}\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}{/tex} E o = 0.34 V {tex}2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{E}^{\circ}=0.00 \mathrm{V}{/tex} since, 2H + /H 2 has lesser Standard electrode Potential than Cu +2 /Cu redox couple. question_text: Conductivity of an electrolytic solution depends on ________. nature of electrolyte. concentration of electrolyte. power of AC source. distance between the electrodes.
question_html:
Conductivity of an electrolytic solution depends on ________.

  1. nature of electrolyte.
  2. concentration of electrolyte.
  3. power of AC source.
  4. distance between the electrodes.
solution_text: The conductivity of the electrolyte solution is due to the presence of mobile ions in the solution. This type of conductance is known as ionic conductance. The conductivity of these type of solutions depend upon the nature of electrolyte added. size of the ion produced and their solvation. the concentration of the electrolyte. The nature of the solvent and its viscosity. temperature ( it increase with the increase in temperature). While the power of source or distance between electrodes has no effect on the conductivity of the electrolyte solution. Hence, options (i) and (ii) are the correct choices.
solution_html:
The conductivity of the electrolyte solution is due to the presence of mobile ions in the solution. This type of conductance is known as ionic conductance. The conductivity of these type of solutions depend upon

  1. the nature of electrolyte added.
  2. size of the ion produced and their solvation.
  3. the concentration of the electrolyte.
  4. The nature of the solvent and its viscosity.
  5. temperature ( it increase with the increase in temperature).

While the power of source or distance between electrodes has no effect on the conductivity of the electrolyte solution. Hence, options (i) and (ii) are the correct choices.

question_text: {tex}\Lambda_{m}^{0} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}{/tex} is equal to ________. {tex}\Lambda_{m\left(\mathrm{HCl}\right)}^{0} +\Lambda_{m(\mathrm{NaOH})}^{0}-\Lambda_{m}^{0}(\mathrm{NaCl}){/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{m\left(\mathrm{HNO}_{3} \mathrm{}\right)}^{0} + \cdot \Lambda_{m\left(\mathrm{NaNO}_{3}\right)}^{0}-\Lambda_{m\left(\mathrm{Na} \mathrm{OH}\right)}^{0}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\left(\mathrm{HNO}_{3}\right)}^{0}+\Lambda_{m_{(\mathrm{NaOH})}}^{0}-\Lambda_{m\left(\mathrm{Na} \mathrm{NO_3}\right)}^{0}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{m\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}\right)}^{0}+\Lambda_{m(\mathrm{HCl})}^{0}-\Lambda_{m\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}\right)}^{0}{/tex}
question_html:
{tex}\Lambda_{m}^{0} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}{/tex} is equal to ________.

  1. {tex}\Lambda_{m\left(\mathrm{HCl}\right)}^{0} +\Lambda_{m(\mathrm{NaOH})}^{0}-\Lambda_{m}^{0}(\mathrm{NaCl}){/tex}
  2. {tex}\Lambda_{m\left(\mathrm{HNO}_{3} \mathrm{}\right)}^{0} + \cdot \Lambda_{m\left(\mathrm{NaNO}_{3}\right)}^{0}-\Lambda_{m\left(\mathrm{Na} \mathrm{OH}\right)}^{0}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\Lambda_{\left(\mathrm{HNO}_{3}\right)}^{0}+\Lambda_{m_{(\mathrm{NaOH})}}^{0}-\Lambda_{m\left(\mathrm{Na} \mathrm{NO_3}\right)}^{0}{/tex}
  4. {tex}\Lambda_{m\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}\right)}^{0}+\Lambda_{m(\mathrm{HCl})}^{0}-\Lambda_{m\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}\right)}^{0}{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}^{\circ}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{HCl})}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NaOH})}^{\circ}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NaCl})}^{\circ}{/tex} {tex} \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)}^{\circ}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)}^{0}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right)}^{0}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\right)}^{0} {/tex} {tex}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)}^{0}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\right)}^{0}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right)}^{0} {/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{HNO}_{3}\right)}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NaOH})}^{\circ}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{NaNO}_{3}\right)}^{\circ}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}\right)}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\right)}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)}^{\circ}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}\right)}^{0}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)}^{0}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{OH}^{*}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}\right)}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{HCl})}^{\mathrm{o}}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} However, the sum of molar conductivities of constituent ions gives the molar conductivity of water but here {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}{/tex} is a weak electrolyte of which complete decomposition is not possible. Therefore (i) and (ii) are the correct options while option (iii) and (iv) are incorrect options.
solution_html:
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}^{\circ}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{HCl})}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NaOH})}^{\circ}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NaCl})}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex} \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)}^{\circ}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)}^{0}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right)}^{0}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\right)}^{0} {/tex}{tex}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)}^{0}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\right)}^{0}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right)}^{0} {/tex}
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{HNO}_{3}\right)}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NaOH})}^{\circ}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{NaNO}_{3}\right)}^{\circ}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex}
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}\right)}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\right)}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)}^{\circ}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex}{tex}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}\right)}^{0}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)}^{0}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{OH}^{*}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex}
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}\right)}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{HCl})}^{\mathrm{o}}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex}
However, the sum of molar conductivities of constituent ions gives the molar conductivity of water but here {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}{/tex} is a weak electrolyte of which complete decomposition is not possible.
Therefore (i) and (ii) are the correct options while option (iii) and (iv) are incorrect options.

At cathode,

solution_text: Under the conditions of electrolysis of aqueous sodium chloride, oxidation of water at anode requires over standard electrode potential and therefore, Cu is oxidised instead of water.

question_text: What is electrode potential?
question_html: What is electrode potential?
solution_text: The electrical potential difference set up between the metal (electrode) and its solution (electrolytes)is called electrode potential.
solution_html: The electrical potential difference set up between the metal (electrode) and its solution (electrolytes)is called electrode potential.

question_text: Why is alternating current used for measuring resistance of an electrolytic solution?
question_html: Why is alternating current used for measuring resistance of an electrolytic solution?

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: A galvanic cell has electrical potential of 1.1V. If an opposing potential of 1.1V is applied to this cell, what will happen to the cell reaction and current flowing through the cell?
question_html: A galvanic cell has electrical potential of 1.1V. If an opposing potential of 1.1V is applied to this cell, what will happen to the cell reaction and current flowing through the cell?
solution_text: When the opposing potential becomes equal to an electrical potential, the cell reaction stops and no current flows through the cell. Thus, there is no chemical reaction to take place in cell.
solution_html: When the opposing potential becomes equal to an electrical potential, the cell reaction stops and no current flows through the cell. Thus, there is no chemical reaction to take place in cell.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: How will the pH of brine (aq. NaCl solution) be affected when it is electrolyzed?
question_html: How will the pH of brine (aq. NaCl solution) be affected when it is electrolyzed?
solution_text: An aqueous solution of brine(aq solution of NaCl) contains Na + , Cl, H +, and H - . Electrode process is given as follows: {tex}2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}{/tex} (At Cathode) {tex}2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}{/tex} (At Anode) The remaining solution will contain NaOH, therefore, pH will increase.
solution_html:
An aqueous solution of brine(aq solution of NaCl) contains Na+, Cl, H+, and H-. Electrode process is given as follows:
{tex}2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}{/tex}(At Cathode)
{tex}2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}{/tex} (At Anode)
The remaining solution will contain NaOH, therefore, pH will increase.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Unlike dry cell, the mercury cell has a constant cell potential throughout its useful life. Why?
question_html: Unlike dry cell, the mercury cell has a constant cell potential throughout its useful life. Why?
solution_text: The lifetime of any cell depends upon the ions ps the cell. The electrolyte is not consumed in the cell process of mercury cell. Hence it will deliver the current at constant potential throughout its life.
solution_html: The lifetime of any cell depends upon the ions ps the cell. The electrolyte is not consumed in the cell process of mercury cell. Hence it will deliver the current at constant potential throughout its life.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Solutions of two electrolytes A and B are diluted. The {tex}\Lambda_{m}{/tex} of B increases 1.5 times while that of A increases 25 times. Which of the two is a strong electrolyte? Justify your answer.
question_html: Solutions of two electrolytes A and B are diluted. The {tex}\Lambda_{m}{/tex} of B increases 1.5 times while that of A increases 25 times. Which of the two is a strong electrolyte? Justify your answer.
solution_text: Electrolyte 'B' will be a strong electrolyte than A because it is completely ionised and on dilution, the molar conductance will increase to a small extent due to an increase in the speed of ions as an interionic attraction decreases.
solution_html: Electrolyte 'B' will be a strong electrolyte than A because it is completely ionised and on dilution, the molar conductance will increase to a small extent due to an increase in the speed of ions as an interionic attraction decreases.

solution_text: The pH of the dil.H ​​2 SO 4 solution will not be affected because there is no change in the concentration of H + ions. At anode: 2 H2O(l) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} O2(g) + 4H+ + 4e-
At cathode: {tex}4 \mathrm{H}^{+}+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \uparrow{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Write the Nernst equation for the cell reaction in the Daniel cell. How will the E Cell be affected when concentration of Zn 2+ ions is increased?
question_html: Write the Nernst equation for the cell reaction in the Daniel cell. How will the ECell be affected when concentration of Zn2+ ions is increased?
solution_text: Nernst equation of Daniel cell {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} \| \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right| \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s}){/tex} {tex}Q=\frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex} Above equation shows that the cell potential will decrease with increase in the concentration of Zn 2+ ion.
solution_html:
Nernst equation of Daniel cell
{tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} \| \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right| \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s}){/tex}

{tex}Q=\frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex}
Above equation shows that the cell potential will decrease with increase in the concentration of Zn2+ ion.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: What advantage do the fuel cells have over primary and secondary batteries?
question_html: What advantage do the fuel cells have over primary and secondary batteries?
solution_text: Primary batteries contain a limited amount of reactants and are discharged when the reactants have been consumed as reactions occur only once. Secondary batteries can be recharged but take a long time to recharge. Fuel cell runs continuously as long as the reactants are supplied to it and as the products are removed continuously.
solution_html: Primary batteries contain a limited amount of reactants and are discharged when the reactants have been consumed as reactions occur only once. Secondary batteries can be recharged but take a long time to recharge. Fuel cell runs continuously as long as the reactants are supplied to it and as the products are removed continuously.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Write the cell reaction of a lead storage battery when it is discharged. How does the density of the electrolyte change when the battery is discharged?
question_html: Write the cell reaction of a lead storage battery when it is discharged. How does the density of the electrolyte change when the battery is discharged?
solution_text: Overall cell reaction consisting of cathode and anode is: Pb + PbO 2 + 2H 2 SO 4 {tex}\stackrel{\text { Discharge }}{\longrightarrow}{/tex} 2PbSO 4 + 2H 2 O The density of electrolytes decreases because water is formed and sulphuric acid consumed as the product during discharge of the battery.
solution_html:
Overall cell reaction consisting of cathode and anode is:
Pb + PbO2 + 2H2SO4 {tex}\stackrel{\text { Discharge }}{\longrightarrow}{/tex} 2PbSO4 + 2H2O
The density of electrolytes decreases because water is formed and sulphuric acid consumed as the product during discharge of the battery.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Why on dilution the m {tex}\Lambda_{m}{/tex} of CH 3 COOH increases drastically, while that of CH 3 COONa increases gradually?
question_html: Why on dilution the m {tex}\Lambda_{m}{/tex}of CH3COOH increases drastically, while that of CH3COONa increases gradually?
solution_text: In the case of CH 3 COOH, which is a weak electrolyte, the number of ions increase on dilution due to an increase in the degree of dissociation. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} {/tex} {tex}\rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}(\mathrm{aq} .)+\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}{/tex} In the case of strong electrolyte such as CH 3 COONa, the number of ions remains the same but the interionic attraction between ions decreases on the dilution.
solution_html:
In the case of CH3COOH, which is a weak electrolyte, the number of ions increase on dilution due to an increase in the degree of dissociation.
{tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} {/tex}{tex}\rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}(\mathrm{aq} .)+\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}{/tex}
In the case of strong electrolyte such as CH3COONa, the number of ions remains the same but the interionic attraction between ions decreases on the dilution.

question_text: Consider the given Figure and answer the following questions. Cell A has E Cell = 2V and Cell B has E Cell = 1.1V. Which of the two cells A or B will act as an electrolytic cell? Which electrode reactions will occur in this cell? If cell A has E Cell = 0.5V and cell B has E Cell = 1.1V then what will be the reactions at anode and cathode?
question_html:
Consider the given Figure and answer the following questions.

  1. Cell A has ECell = 2V and Cell B has ECell = 1.1V. Which of the two cells A or B will act as an electrolytic cell? Which electrode reactions will occur in this cell?
  2. If cell A has ECell = 0.5V and cell B has ECell = 1.1V then what will be the reactions at anode and cathode?
solution_text: Cell 'B' will act as an electrolytic cell because the electrode potential of 'B' is less than that of 'A'. Electrode process in the cell 'B' may be given as Zn 2+ + 2e - {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} Zn(s) (at Cathode) Cu (s ) {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} Cu 2+ + 2e - (at Anode ) Cell 'B' at higher potential will act as the galvanic cell. The electrode process may be given as, At anode: Zn (s) {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} Zn 2+ +2e - At Cathode Cu 2+ +2e - {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} Cu (s)
solution_html:
1. Cell 'B' will act as an electrolytic cell because the electrode potential of 'B' is less than that of 'A'. Electrode process in the cell 'B' may be given as
    Zn2+ + 2e- {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} Zn(s) (at Cathode)
    Cu (s ) {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} Cu2+ + 2e- (at Anode )
  2. Cell 'B' at higher potential will act as the galvanic cell. The electrode process may be given as,
    At anode: Zn (s) {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} Zn2+ +2e-
    At Cathode Cu2+ +2e- {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} Cu (s)

question_text: Consider figure given and answer the questions (i) to (vi) given below. Redraw the diagram to show the direction of electron flow. Is silver plate the anode or cathode? What will happen if salt bridge is removed? When will the cell stop functioning? How will concentration of Zn 2+ ions and Ag + ions be affected when the cell functions? How will the concentration of Zn 2+ ions and Ag + ions be affected after the cell becomes ‘dead’?
question_html:
Consider figure given and answer the questions (i) to (vi) given below.

  1. Redraw the diagram to show the direction of electron flow.
  2. Is silver plate the anode or cathode?
  3. What will happen if salt bridge is removed?
  4. When will the cell stop functioning?
  5. How will concentration of Zn2+ ions and Ag+ ions be affected when the cell functions?
  6. How will the concentration of Zn2+ ions and Ag+ ions be affected after the cell becomes ‘dead’?
solution_text: The cell is: {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{+2} \| \mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right| \mathrm{Ag}{/tex} The electron will flow from zinc anode to silver cathode in an external circuit. Silver will act as a cathode, since its standard reduction potential is greater than that of zinc. Potential will drop to zero if the salt bridge is suddenly removed. The cell will stop functioning when it is discharged i.e., when cell potential is zero. The concentration of Zn 2+ and Ag + affected as Nernst equation for the cell is: 0.059 {tex}E=E^{0}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\left[Z n^{2+}\right]}{\left[A g^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} Cell potential will decrease with an increase in the concentration of [Zn 2+ ] while it will increase with the concentration of [Ag + ]. When a cell is dead or discharged, E will be zero and the cell will be at equilibrium. Then, the concentration of {tex}\mathrm{Zn}^{+2}{/tex} and Ag + will not change.
solution_html:
1. The cell is:
    {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{+2} \| \mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right| \mathrm{Ag}{/tex}
  2. The electron will flow from zinc anode to silver cathode in an external circuit. Silver will act as a cathode, since its standard reduction potential is greater than that of zinc.
  3. Potential will drop to zero if the salt bridge is suddenly removed.
  4. The cell will stop functioning when it is discharged i.e., when cell potential is zero.
  5. The concentration of Zn2+ and Ag+ affected as
  6. Nernst equation for the cell is: 0.059
    {tex}E=E^{0}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\left[Z n^{2+}\right]}{\left[A g^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
    Cell potential will decrease with an increase in the concentration of [Zn2+] while it will increase with the concentration of [Ag+].
  7. When a cell is dead or discharged, E will be zero and the cell will be at equilibrium. Then,  the concentration of {tex}\mathrm{Zn}^{+2}{/tex} and Ag+ will not change.

question_text: What is the relationship between Gibbs free energy of the cell reaction in a galvanic cell and the emf of the cell? When will the maximum work be obtained from a galvanic cell?
question_html: What is the relationship between Gibbs free energy of the cell reaction in a galvanic cell and the emf of the cell? When will the maximum work be obtained from a galvanic cell?
solution_text: The required relations are: {tex}\Delta G{/tex} = -nFE W max = nFE o where, E = Cell potential, n= no. of electrons E o = Standard emf of the cell
solution_html:
The required relations are:
{tex}\Delta G{/tex} = -nFE
Wmax = nFEo

Eo = Standard emf of the cell
